mnemonics: Density-Based Clustering: Objects Are Neighbors (DBSCAN) - Points are grouped into clusters based on how closely they're packed together, like neighbors in a dense city. Core points have many neighbors, border points have fewer, and noise points are isolated.
keyPoints: DBSCAN forms clusters based on dense regions of points, separated by areas of lower point density.

It defines core points (points with at least MinPts neighbors within a radius ε), border points (points within ε of a core point but without enough neighbors themselves), and noise points (points that are neither).
Unlike centroid-based methods like K-Means, DBSCAN can find clusters of arbitrary shape and does not require specifying the number of clusters beforehand.
commonMistakes: Improperly tuning the ε and MinPts parameters, which can lead to all points being labeled as a single cluster or as noise.
Assuming the algorithm will perform well on data with clusters of widely varying densities, as a single global ε parameter may not be suitable for all clusters.
